coredata.dc:title: Modification of microencapsulated phase change materials(MPCMs) by synthesizing graphene quantum dots(GQDs) and nano-aluminum for energy storage and heat transfer applications
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Zhongzhu
Zhou, Yufei
Yao, Yuan
Liu, Fang
Guo, Ruitang
coredata.dc:description: MPCMs and their suspensions, acting as the thermal storage, heat transfer or temperature constancy mediums, have gained concerns in various energy related sectors. However, problems involving high supercooling degree, low thermal conductivity and suspensions instability are barriers for their energy storage applications. The present study focuses on such properties by adding GQDs/nano-aluminum into MPCMs particles. Paraffin was selected as core material and urea–melamine–formaldehyde polymer as shell to prepare 10 MPCMs samples (no modifiers, GQDs, nano-aluminum, GQDs & nano-aluminum) via in situ polymerization. The morphology, thermal conductivity, thermal property and MPCM/suspensions stability were characterized. It was found, the selected modifiers didn't impact on the microcapsules morphology, but GQDs can make particle size smaller and distribution more uniform. Adversely, the mean particle size can be increased by nano-aluminum. GQDs are much more effective than nano-aluminum in improving thermal conductivity. GQDs can suppress supercooling effectively, however, nano-aluminum has no obvious effect on supercooling suppression. The MPCMs suspension modified by the selected amount of GQDs and nano-aluminum (Al-GQDs(4.5–2)) achieved a high physical stability. No structure instability of modified MPCM sample was observed. To sum up, the combined effort of GQDs and nano-aluminum enabled MPCMs to be more applicable in energy storage applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microencapsulated phase change material
Graphene quantum dots
Nano-aluminum
Multiple properties
Modification

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Stabilities of MPCM suspensions; 1. Suspension tank, 2. Pump, 3. Electrical heater, 4. Plate heat exchanger(evaporator),5. Compressor, 6. Fan coiler, 7. Expansion valve.
Effect of GQDs, heat rate is 5 °C/min.
Effect of nano-aluminum, heat rate is 5 °C/min.
Effect of compositing both nano-aluminum and GQDs, heat rate is 5 °C/min.

Stabilities of MPCM suspensions.
（2）Structure(thermal) stability of MPCM suspensions
SEM pictures of MPCM before and after one-week thermal cycling.

Materials.
•
0.45 g of urea, 4 g of melamine and 9.5 ml of formalin were mixed with 20 ml deionized water, adjust pH to 8.0 by adding triethanolamine and stirred for 30 min at 70 °C, the prepolymer solution was obtained.
•
The prepolymer solution was added into the PCM emulsion in droplets while the emulsion was stirred at a rate of 1000 rpm. The temperature of mixed emulsion was increased to 70 °C and kept being stirred for 120min, which can help the crosslinking polymerization occur.
•
Thereafter, emulsion pH-value was adjusted to 8.5 using triethanolamine and cooled down to room temperature from 70 °C.
•
Finally, MPCM capsules were obtained after filtering, washing and drying.

row.entry: Sodium salt of styrene–maleic anhydride copolymer emulsion (SMA)
Nanjing Yin Xin Chemical Co., Ltd.
Shanghai Wei Yu mechanical and Electrical Co., Ltd.
Shanghai God Precision Instrument Co. Ltd.
Shanghai DongXi refrigeration equipment Co. Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermal conductivity and increase rate of MPCMs.

head.ce:title: Modification of microencapsulated phase change materials(MPCMs) by synthesizing graphene quantum dots(GQDs) and nano-aluminum for energy storage and heat transfer applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Energy and Mechanical Engineering, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai, 200090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Energy and Mechanical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Energy and Mechanical Engineering, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 200090, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MPCMs and their suspensions, acting as the thermal storage, heat transfer or temperature constancy mediums, have gained concerns in various energy related sectors. However, problems involving high supercooling degree, low thermal conductivity and suspensions instability are barriers for their energy storage applications. The present study focuses on such properties by adding GQDs/nano-aluminum into MPCMs particles. Paraffin was selected as core material and urea–melamine–formaldehyde polymer as shell to prepare 10 MPCMs samples (no modifiers, GQDs, nano-aluminum, GQDs & nano-aluminum) via in situ polymerization. The morphology, thermal conductivity, thermal property and MPCM/suspensions stability were characterized. It was found, the selected modifiers didn't impact on the microcapsules morphology, but GQDs can make particle size smaller and distribution more uniform. Adversely, the mean particle size can be increased by nano-aluminum. GQDs are much more effective than nano-aluminum in improving thermal conductivity. GQDs can suppress supercooling effectively, however, nano-aluminum has no obvious effect on supercooling suppression. The MPCMs suspension modified by the selected amount of GQDs and nano-aluminum (Al-GQDs(4.5–2)) achieved a high physical stability. No structure instability of modified MPCM sample was observed. To sum up, the combined effort of GQDs and nano-aluminum enabled MPCMs to be more applicable in energy storage applications.

ce:section.ce:para: MPCM is a new material encapsulated in dense and flexible polymer shell material by microcapsule preparation technology [1]. The energy storage density of phase change materials is large, and the storage and release of heat is realized by phase change caused by the change of external temperature [2,3]. But it has not been widely applied in engineering applications due to its own shortcomings, involving in low thermal conductivity, and suspensions instability.

Yu et al. [4] fabricated series of n-octadecane microcapsules with CaCO3 shell which can deliver a high thermal conductivity. Zhang et al. [5] mixed the n-octadecane and Tetraethylorthosilicate at different ratios at room temperature and the MPCM was prepared by polymerization, whose shell material contains silicon. It was found that the thermal conductivity of the MPCM increased with the increase of the percentage of silicon material. Yamagishi [6] selected n-octadecane as core and melamine-formaldehyde as shell material to prepare MPCM and MPCM suspension. The thermal conductivity of MPCM suspension was higher than that of microcapsule particles. In order to improve the thermal conductivity of the phase change material, some additives are needed. Fan et al. [7] summarized the physical properties of various graphite and metal additives, the thermal conductivity of multi-wall carbon nanotubes is up to 4000w/m
⋅
K. Li et al. [8] added 4 wt% grafted carbon nanotubes into paraffin wax, and consequently the thermal conductivity of paraffin wax increased from 0.2312 w/m K to 0.7903 w/m K. Shi et al. [9] added 10 wt% graphene into paraffin wax, therefore increasing its thermal conductivity by 200%.

Salunkhe et al. [10] found that particles size, nucleating agents and their addition ratio can influence supercooling degree of MPCM. Cao et al. [11] selected sodium chloride with addition ratio of 6% as nucleating agent and suppress supercooling of n-octadecane MPCM effectively. Alvarado et al. [12] chose tetradecyl alcohol as nucleating agent and found it can help decrease the supercooling degree of tetradecane MPCM. Zhang et al. [13] mixed multi-walled carbon nanotube with paraffin wax and suppressed supercooling effectively. Zhang et al. [14] added 1- eighteen alcohol with weight ratio of 9.1% into n-octadecane to eliminate the supercooling of the MPCM, the microcapsules presented low supercooling degree but with inferior morphology and dispersion.

Energy storage systems using phase change materials have been widely used in different areas [15]. Two-phase fluid composed of phase change material and carrier fluid is called latent thermal functional fluid. Inaba [16] classified latent heat functional fluids into five types in terms of characteristics and their applications, namely, ice slurry, latent heat microemulsion, latent heat microcapsule, clathrate slurry and shape-stabilized latent heat material. MPCM suspension, one of latent heat functional liquid, is made of MPCM and carrier fluid. It has many advantages when used as heat transfer media, such as high specific heat capacity, high surface area and high heat transfer rate [17–19]. However, its poor physical stability is a barrier for engineering application. The physical instability results from density difference between MPCM particles and carrier fluid. The physical stability of MPCM suspension can be improved by adding surfactants or adjusting the pH of carrier fluid [20,21]. Instability of MPCM suspension was not observed in Wang et al.’s [20] investigation. Such MPCM suspension was fabricated by minimizing density of fluid to equal solid density. Reduction of the density difference between MPCM particles and carrier fluid was achieved by adding modified materials into MPCM.

Previously, only one kind of additive is usually selected to modify an associated property of MPCM. GQDs deliver good thermal and chemical stability, water solubility and mechanical flexibility [22], but has not been investigated as MPCM additive in terms of authors’ review. In current work, however, GQDs and nano-aluminum are combined at a predesigned ratio into MPCM capsules during fabrication to modify multiple properties, involving the particles size and its uniformity, thermal conductivity, encapsulation efficiency, supercooling and stability of MPCM/suspensions.
The materials for fabricating MPCM capsules(core and shell materials) and associated emulsifiers and additives are presented in Table 1 .

In the experiment, urea-melamine-formaldehyde multipolymer and paraffin were selected as wall material and core material respectively. The process of preparing MPCM by in-situ polymerization were schematically shown in Fig. 1 and detailed as below.
•
20 g of paraffin, 4 g of SMA and a predetermined amount of modifier (see Table .1) were mixed with 200 mL deionized water, emulsifying using a mixer (BME 100lx, Shanghai Wei Yu mechanical and Electrical Co., Ltd.) for 120 min at a turning speed of 6000 rpm at 70 °C, then the mixture's pH value was adjusted to 5.5 by adding citric acid. The PCM emulsion was obtained.

Table 2 shows the experimental design, sample A was fabricated with no modifiers, sample B-D were fabricated with GQDs, sample E-G were fabricated with nano-aluminum, sample H-J were fabricated with both GQDs and nano-aluminum.
The devices/instruments for fabricating MPCM capsules and testing physical and thermal properties are presented in Table 3 .

Surface morphology of the microcapsule was observed using a scanning electronic microscope (SEM, JEOL, JSM-7800F). Particle size and its distribution was tested by use of Laser particle size analyzer (Bettersize2000). Thermal conductivity was measured on Thermal conductivity meter (Hot Disk TPS 2500s), ambient temperature upon testing was 25 °C and measuring duration was 10 s.

The phase change properties of PCM and MPCMs were investigated using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, Mettler-Toledo TGA/DSC2) in the temperature range of 45–75 °C with a heating and cooling rate of 5.0 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere.

MPCM suspensions were fabricated by dispersing MPCM particles fabricated in terms of Table 1 into water and creaming phenomenon of prepared suspensions was observed to evaluate the physical stability of different MPCM samples suspensions.

A test rig was set up to evaluate the structure stability of the modified MPCM capsules(Fig. 2 ). It consists of two loops, i.e., MPCM suspension loop and heat pump loop. A thermal cycle can be fulfilled as below: A certain amount of prefabricated MPCM suspension at room temperature was put into the suspension tank, making the suspension loop full of suspension, then it was propelled to flow along the pipe once the centrifugal pump starts to work, meanwhile, it can be heated up to a desired temperature by the electrical heater, and cooled down to the room temperature in the evaporator of the heat pump loop, thereafter it flows back to the tank. The multiple thermal cycles testing can be realized by keeping the pump, electrical heater and heat pump working.
MPCM suspension loop Heat pump loop.

Fig. 3 shows surface morphology of ten fabricated MPCMs. It was found from Fig. 3 that all microcapsules show perfect spheres in shape, indicating that the selected modifiers have no obvious impact on the appearance of microcapsules.

Four samples were fabricated at the condition of increasing amount of GQDs addition in the range of 1–3 wt% to evaluate the effect of GQDs. Their size distribution were analyzed using Laser particle size analyzer, and the results were provided in Fig. 4 (a), (b), (c) and (d). It can be found from Fig. 4 (a), (b), (c) and (d), the particles size distribution curve of unmodified microcapsule looks short and ‘fat’, comparatively, such curve became high and ‘thin’, and the mean particle size decreased from 7.19
μm
to 3.25
μm
when the amount of GQDs addition increased in the range of 1–3 wt%, indicating GQDs enabled the more uniform size distribution and made the mean diameter smaller. It maybe because GQDs could suppress the cohesion among core materials.

Three samples were fabricated at the condition of increasing amount of nano-aluminum addition in the range of 1–3 wt% to evaluate the effect of nano-aluminum. Their size distribution were presented in Fig. 4 (e), (f) and (g). Fig. 4 (a), (b), (c) and (d) show, adverse to the effect of GQDs, the mean particle size of microcapsule modified by nano-aluminum increased from 8.93
μm
to 11.67
μm
. It may be because nano-aluminum particles are not as tiny as GQDs.

Three samples were fabricated at the condition of increasing amount of nano-aluminum addition in the range of 4–5 wt% and keeping amount of GQDs addition 2% unchanged to evaluate the effect of nano-aluminum and GQDs. Their size distribution were presented in Fig. 4 (h), (i) and (j). The results showed the mean particle size of microcapsule modified by GQDs and nano-aluminum increased from 4.62
μm
to 6.87
μm
. In summary, it was found that GQDs influence uniformity of particle size and nano-aluminum has impact on particle size.

Table 4 shows the testing results of thermal conductivity of above-mentioned ten MPCMs samples. It can be found that nano-aluminum can improve thermal conductivity significantly, in comparison, GQDs are much more effective in improving thermal conductivity, e.g., the thermal conductivity of GQDs modified microcapsule is 2 times as much as that of nano-aluminum modified microcapsules when both additions are same as 3 wt%.

DSC curves of MPCMs samples Al-GQDs(0-0), Al-GQDs(0–1), Al-GQDs(0–2), and Al-GQDs(0–3) were presented in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5 shows the melting point of the sample Al-GQDs(0-0), representing unmodified MPCM, is 60.96 °C, the highest one among the four samples. GQDs' addition can reduce MPCM's melting temperature. This is because GQDs improved MPCMs' thermal conductivity.

The supercooling degree of unmodified MPCM(Al-GQDs(0-0)) is the highest one, up to 13.50 °C.GQDs addition of 1%, 2% and 3% reduced such supercooling degree to 9.36 °C, 6.8 °C and 4.98 °C respectively.

DSC curves of MPCMs samples Al-GQDs(0-0), Al-GQDs(1-0), Al-GQDs(2-0), and Al-GQDs(3-0) were presented in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6 shows compositing nano-aluminum can reduce MPCM's melting temperature too. This is also because nano-aluminum improved MPCMs' thermal conductivity.

However, nano-aluminum has no obvious effect on supercooling degree. This may be because GQDs has ultra-high thermal conductivity enabling great heat transfer enhancement and small enough size to be a good nucleating agent making crystallization earlier.

The combined effort of nano-aluminum and GQDs can be evaluated by analyses on DSC curves of the samples Al-GQDs(0-0), Al-GQDs(4-2), Al-GQDs(4.5–2), and Al-GQDs(5-2). DSC curves of MPCMs samples Al-GQDs(0-0), Al-GQDs(4-2), Al-GQDs(4.5–2), and Al-GQDs(5-2), and Al-GQDs(3-0) were presented in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7 shows compositing both nano-aluminum and GQDs can reduce MPCM's melting temperature and supercooling degree significantly.

MPCM suspension can be fabricated by dispersing microcapsules into water together with some additives, MPCM suspensions whose mass fraction were 10% was selected for current investigation [23–25]. The suspension fabrication steps were: dispersing MPCM capsules into water, then, the suspension was stirred mechanically at a rate of 1000 rpm for 3 h, thereafter, ultrasonically dispersed for 20 min in 50HZ. Subsequently, the suspensions were poured into the test tubes and kept stay statically for 0 h, 24 h and 48 h. The observation results were presented in Fig. 8 .

It was observed from Fig. 8(ii) that GQDs modified microcapsule suspensions B, C and D showed more obvious creaming than unmodified and nano-aluminum modified microcapsule suspensions. This is because nano-aluminum is much denser than GQDs, thus it can help reduce the density gap between microcapsule particles and water, enabling the more stable suspensions.

It was found from Fig. 8 (iii) that suspensions A-H and J showed obvious creaming or sedimentation after 48 h. Comparatively, the suspension ‘I’ was not observed any instability within 48 h, indicating a better physical stability.

The sample (J)Al-GQDs(5-2) presented in Table 2 was selected to evaluate the structure stability of the MPCM. The testing rig have been kept operating for one week, indicating that MPCM capsules may have finished more than 60,000 thermal cycles in terms of estimation.

SEM pictures of MPCM before and after one-week thermal cycling is provided in Fig. 9 . No structure instability(break-up) of MPCM microcapsules was found in Fig. 9 (b), indicating neither nano-aluminum or GQDs impacts on the structure stability of the MPCM microcapsules.

MPCMs compositing the selected modifiers GQDs and nano-aluminum were successfully prepared by in-situ polymerization process. The morphology, size, thermal conductivity, melting point, supercooling and physical stability of MPCM suspensions were observed and/or analyzed.

It was found, the selected modifiers didn't impact on the microcapsules morphology, but GQDs can make particle size distribution more uniform, and mean diameter smaller. Adversely, the mean particle size can be increased by nano-aluminum.

Nano-aluminum can improve thermal conductivity significantly, in comparison, GQDs are much more effective in improving thermal conductivity. Both GQDs' and nano-aluminum can reduce MPCM's melting temperature because they can improve MPCM's thermal conductivity significantly.

GQDs can suppress supercooling effectively, however, nano-aluminum has no obvious effect on supercooling degree. This may be because GQDs has ultra-high thermal conductivity enabling great heat transfer enhancement and small enough size to be a good nucleating agent making crystallization earlier. Compositing both nano-aluminum and GQDs can reduce MPCM's melting temperature and supercooling degree significantly.
The MPCM suspension modified by the selected amount of GQDs and nano-aluminum (Al-GQDs(4.5–2)) was not observed any creaming or sedimentation within 48 h, indicating a high physical stability.
Thermal cycling testing indicated that either nano-aluminum or GQDs impacts on the structure stability of the MPCM microcapsules.
In conclusion, microcapsule modified by the selected amount of GQDs and nano-aluminum delivered uniform particles size, high thermal conductivity and low suspension supercooling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would acknowledge our appreciation of financial supports from the EU Marie Curie Actions-International Incoming Fellowships (FP7-PEOPLE-2011-IIF-298093), Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai, China(17ZR1411300), and Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality(17DZ1201500).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of materials, heat transfer and phase change problem formulation for latent heat thermal energy storage systems (LHTESS)
